result.markdown:
--- title: "Group analysis" subtitle: "Second-level inference over first-level estimates, fully typed." jupyter: python3 execute: freeze: auto --- The production spine ends here: per-subject first-level estimates flow into a typed `GroupData` container and out through typed result objects (`FmriMetaResult`, `GroupFitResult`, `FmriTTestResult`). The group design is a formula, not a hand-built `np.column_stack`; the outputs are typed attributes, not dictionary keys. ## Per-Subject Estimates → Typed `GroupData` A second-level analysis starts from one effect (and its standard error) per subject per feature. Stack them as a tidy frame and let `group_data_from_csv` build the typed container — it accepts an in-memory DataFrame and a covariate table, so the group design is declared by data, not assembled by hand. ::: {#bc65240a .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import fmrimod as fm from fmrimod.dataset import group_data_from_csv from fmrimod.stats import fmri_meta, group_fit, GroupFitRequest, fmri_ttest rng = np.random.default_rng(12) n_subj, n_feat = 10, 6 subjects = [f"s{i + 1}" for i in range(n_subj)] group = np.repeat(["ctrl", "patient"], n_subj // 2) # Synthetic first-level betas + sampling variances (the fixture only). true = np.array([[0.2] * n_feat, [0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.0, -0.1, -0.2]]) V = rng.uniform(0.04, 0.12, size=(n_subj, n_feat)) gnum = (group == "patient").astype(int) Y = np.column_stack([np.ones(n_subj), gnum]) @ true + rng.normal(scale=np.sqrt(V)) long = pd.DataFrame([ {"subject": s, "roi": f"feature_{j + 1}", "beta": Y[i, j], "se": np.sqrt(V[i, j])} for i, s in enumerate(subjects) for j in range(n_feat) ]) gd = group_data_from_csv( long, effect_cols={"beta": "beta", "se": "se"}, subject_col="subject", roi_col="roi", covariates=pd.DataFrame({"group": group}), ) gd.format, len(gd.subjects) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` ('csv', 10) ``` ::: ::: ## One-Sample Random Effects The common second-level question — "is the effect non-zero across subjects?" — is a random-effects meta-analysis with an intercept-only formula. `fmri_meta` returns a typed `FmriMetaResult`: read `.coefficients`, `.p`, `.tau2` as attributes. ::: {#de79b8d0 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} one = fmri_meta(gd, formula="~ 1", method="pm") pd.DataFrame({ "feature": one.feature_names, "estimate": one.coefficients[:, 0], "p": one.p[:, 0], "tau2": one.tau2, }).round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
feature estimate p tau2
0 roi=feature_1 0.412 0.000 0.042
1 roi=feature_2 0.337 0.000 0.001
2 roi=feature_3 0.302 0.001 0.000
3 roi=feature_4 0.256 0.011 0.018
4 roi=feature_5 0.100 0.245 0.004
5 roi=feature_6 -0.044 0.617 0.000
``` ::: ::: ## The Canonical Typed Entry: `group_fit` `group_fit` is the single second-level interface. You describe the analysis with a frozen `GroupFitRequest` and get a frozen `GroupFitResult` back — the same numbers as `fmri_meta`, but through the parity-normalized seam the flagship workflows use. ::: {#792bed9f .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} res = group_fit( GroupFitRequest( data=gd, formula="~ 1", model="meta", effects="random", tau2="pm", ) ) type(res).__name__, res.method, res.backend, res.estimate[:, 0].round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` ('GroupFitResult', 'pm', 'python', array([ 0.412, 0.337, 0.302, 0.256, 0.1 , -0.044])) ``` ::: ::: ## Between-Group Meta-Regression To test a group difference, add the covariate to the formula. The fixed-effects path supports covariate designs; the coefficient for `C(group)[T.patient]` is the between-group contrast per feature. ::: {#158ac0b3 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} reg = fmri_meta(gd, formula="~ 1 + C(group)", method="fe") pd.DataFrame({ "feature": reg.feature_names, "patient_minus_ctrl": reg.coefficients[:, 1], "p": reg.p[:, 1], }).round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
feature patient_minus_ctrl p
0 roi=feature_1 0.453 0.004
1 roi=feature_2 0.299 0.096
2 roi=feature_3 0.138 0.453
3 roi=feature_4 0.278 0.123
4 roi=feature_5 -0.169 0.307
5 roi=feature_6 -0.129 0.461
``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note} The typed **random-effects** path is intercept-only today: a covariate formula with `method="pm"`/`"dl"`/`"reml"` raises `NotImplementedError`. This is a tracked parity gap, not a statistical limitation — the R oracle implements it (`fmrigds` `meta:re_reg`, DerSimonian–Laird tau²), and porting it is the documented exit criterion (`typed-re-meta-regression-unimplemented` in [`docs/contracts/CAVEATS.md`](https://github.com/bbuchsbaum/fmrimod/blob/main/docs/contracts/CAVEATS.md)). Until then, use `method="fe"` for covariate meta-regression, or the `fmri_meta_fit(Y, V, X, ...)` matrix compat helper for random-effects with covariates. ::: ## One-Sample Test, One Call For a plain one-sample second-level *t*-test, `fmri_ttest` is the typed shortcut — same `GroupData`, a `FmriTTestResult` out. ::: {#8b41fbb9 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} tt = fmri_ttest(gd, formula="~ 1") type(tt).__name__, tt.engine, tt.statistic.round(2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` ('FmriTTestResult', 'meta', array([ 3.99, 3.75, 3.3 , 2.56, 1.16, -0.5 ])) ``` ::: ::: ## Next - [Functional connectivity](functional-connectivity.qmd) - [Benchmark datasets](benchmark-datasets.qmd)
